description:
You are a junior compliance-analysis engineer.  
    Your manager has asked you to generate a reproducible, machine-readable audit trail of every
    document beneath “/home/user/compliance” that **contains the exact, case-sensitive
    substring “PCI-DSS” at least once**.

    1. Search scope  
       • Root directory to scan:  /home/user/compliance  
       • Include only regular files whose extension is “.txt” or “.md”.  
       • Follow the directory tree recursively; do **not** traverse symbolic links.  

    2. What must be extracted for each matching file  
       a) The full absolute path to the file (no relative paths).  
       b) The SHA-256 digest of the whole file, exactly as produced by the Linux
          utility “sha256sum” (lower-case hex, 64 chars, no leading “0x”).  
       c) The number of times the literal token “PCI-DSS” occurs in that file
          (case sensitive, so “pci-dss” does **not** count).  
          The counter must be an unsigned integer (e.g. 3, not “three” or “03”).  

    3. Log-file requirements  
       • Location:   /home/user/audit/pci_audit_log.csv  
       • The /home/user/audit/ directory may not exist yet; create it if necessary.  
       • The file must be valid CSV with **exactly** these three columns:
         “file_path”, “sha256”, “pci_dss_mentions” (spelled exactly, all lower case).  
       • A single header line is mandatory and must be followed by one data line
         for each qualifying file.  
       • Column separator: a single comma.  
       • Do **not** surround fields with quotes, even if they contain “/”.  
       • Lines must be separated with a single Unix newline character “\n”; no
         trailing blank lines.  
       • File order: sort the data lines alphabetically by the full file path
         (ascending, byte-wise).  

       Example of the required format (values shown are only illustrative):

       file_path,sha256,pci_dss_mentions  
       /home/user/compliance/2021/example.txt,aa11bb22…,2  
       /home/user/compliance/2022/doc.md,bb22cc33…,1  

    4. Performance constraint  
       You must demonstrate that you can perform the SHA-256 and counting steps in
       parallel.  Use a single “find … | xargs -P” (or equivalent) pipeline that
       executes at least **four parallel** worker processes.  
       The audit department will inspect your history, so produce everything via
       non-interactive shell commands rather than editing files manually.  

    5. Deliverables  
       • /home/user/audit/pci_audit_log.csv that conforms **exactly** to the
         format described above.  
       • A compressed copy of the same log named
         /home/user/audit/pci_audit_log.csv.gz created with “gzip -9”.  

    6. Verification notes  
       The automated grading script will:  
       1. Re-scan /home/user/compliance for every file that contains “PCI-DSS”
          and has the extension .txt or .md.  
       2. For each match it will independently compute the SHA-256 digest and
          token count.  
       3. It will read your CSV, validate the header, rows, order and field
          syntax, and then compare every value with its own calculations.  
       4. It will gunzip your .gz copy and ensure the decompressed text is a byte
         -for-byte match to the original csv.  

    7. Hints (NOT commands!)  
       • “grep -c”, “awk ‘{print …}’”, and “sha256sum” might be useful together
         with “xargs -I{} -P4 …”.  
       • Remember that “grep -c” counts lines that match at least once; you need
         total token occurrences.  “grep -o” can help.  
       • Keep the header out of the sort operation so it stays on the first line.
truth:
<!--
        Initial file tree that already exists when the task starts.
        All files use Unix newlines and end with a final “\n”.

        1) /home/user/compliance/2021/payments_policy.txt
           Payments Processing Policy
           Revision: 2021-03-15

           All cardholder data is handled in accordance with PCI-DSS v3.2.
           We mandate quarterly scans to satisfy PCI-DSS requirements.
           End of document.

           PCI-DSS occurrences: 2

        2) /home/user/compliance/2021/archive/old_payment.md
           Legacy system documentation referencing PCI-DSS.

           PCI-DSS occurrences: 1

        3) /home/user/compliance/2022/network_controls.md
           # Network Security Controls
           The firewall configuration complies with PCI-DSS guidelines.
           Traffic is segmented based on PCI-DSS zones.

           PCI-DSS occurrences: 2

        4) /home/user/compliance/2022/README.txt
           This directory contains network control documentation.
           (0 occurrences – must NOT appear in the final csv)

        5) /home/user/compliance/2023/draft/pci_overview.md
           # PCI Overview Draft
           PCI-DSS scope determination is critical.
           PCI-DSS scope includes all systems that store, process, or transmit cardholder data.
           PCI-DSS!

           PCI-DSS occurrences: 3

        Alphabetical path order (which the csv must use):
          /home/user/compliance/2021/archive/old_payment.md
          /home/user/compliance/2021/payments_policy.txt
          /home/user/compliance/2022/network_controls.md
          /home/user/compliance/2023/draft/pci_overview.md

        The grader will calculate sha256 sums at runtime; thus only the paths
        and counts are required here for correctness checks.
    -->
    <pci_files>
        <file path="/home/user/compliance/2021/archive/old_payment.md" mentions="1"/>
        <file path="/home/user/compliance/2021/payments_policy.txt" mentions="2"/>
        <file path="/home/user/compliance/2022/network_controls.md" mentions="2"/>
        <file path="/home/user/compliance/2023/draft/pci_overview.md" mentions="3"/>
    </pci_files>
    <non_matching>
        /home/user/compliance/2022/README.txt
    </non_matching>
    <expected_csv>/home/user/audit/pci_audit_log.csv</expected_csv>
    <expected_gz>/home/user/audit/pci_audit_log.csv.gz</expected_gz>
